journal-title-group.journal-title: The European Journal of Public Health

subj-group.subject: 3.S. Poster walk: Monitoring population health: surveilance and responses

title-group.article-title: Subjective memory impairment and long COVID: findings from the German Health Update 2023 Survey

contrib.aff: Epidemiology and Health Monitoring, Robert Koch Institute, Berlin, Germany

All other permissions can be obtained through our RightsLink service via the Permissions link on the article page on our site—for further information please contact journals.permissions@oup.com.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Long COVID (LC) comprises manifold long-term health consequences following SARS-CoV-2 infection, posing significant public health challenges. Major health complaints include cognitive impairment, such as memory or concentration problems. This contribution examines subjective memory impairment (SMI) in association with self-reported LC among adults in Germany.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: The study population consisted of 9.296 adults aged 18 years or older from the nationwide cross-sectional telephone German Health Update 2023 Survey (GEDA 2023, March 2023-February 2024), with information on self-reported prior SARS-CoV-2 infection and self-reported previous or current LC. SMI was defined as self-reported memory worsening with associated worries. We report weighted SMI prevalence by self-reported LC status and effect estimates from logistic regression, adjusted for sex, age, education level, partnership status, chronic conditions, depressive symptoms and social support.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Overall, 16.1% (95% CI: 14.9-17.3) reported having SMI. Individuals with SARS-CoV-2 infection and current LC stated SMI more often (38.6% [31.8-45.5]) compared to individuals with infection and previous LC (17.3% [12.4-22.2]), participants with infection and no LC (13.8% [12.2-15.4]) or non-infected participants (15% [13.2-16.9]). In a multivariable regression model, current LC was associated with higher risk for SMI compared with non-infected participants (adjusted relative risk: 2.22). Previous LC or prior infection without LC had no significant effect.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Self-reported current LC was associated with a more than 2-fold higher risk of SMI compared with the non-infected group - irrespective of further known risks for cognitive decline like higher age or depressive symptoms. Further research is needed with regard to the duration of LC-associated memory impairment and its impact on everyday functioning and quality of life.

sec.title: Key messages
sec.p: • Self-reported LC is associated with a substantially higher risk of SMI compared to non-infected individuals – irrespective of further known risks for cognitive decline.
• Our findings emphasize existing evidence on the significance of cognitive impairment as LC-associated core symptom.
